blocks:
Ayahs

يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah. To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise, and He has power over all things. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ فَمِنكُمْ كَافِرٌ وَمِنكُم مُّؤْمِنٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢)
It is He who created you. Then some of you are faithless and some of you are faithful, and Allah watches what you do. (2)
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٣)
He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom, and He formed you and perfected your forms, and toward Him is your destination. (3)
يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٤)
He knows whatever there is in the heavens and the earth, and He knows whatever you hide and whatever you disclose, and Allah knows best what is in your breasts. (4)
أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ فَذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٥)
Has there not come to you the account of those who were faithless before? They tasted the evil consequence of their conduct, and there is a painful punishment for them. (5)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُۥ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنَا فَكَفَرُوا۟ وَتَوَلَّوا۟ ۚ وَّٱسْتَغْنَى ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (٦)
That was because their apostles would bring them manifest proofs, but they said, ‘Will humans be our guides?!’ So they disbelieved and turned away, and Allah had no need [of their faith] and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable. (6)

Content of Surah At-Taghabun

There is intense debate among commentators as to whether this Surah was revealed in Medina or Mecca. Although the popular view is that it is Medinan, some consider it entirely Meccan, while others believe only the last three verses are Medinan and the rest are Meccan.

Certainly, the tone of the final verses of this Surah is consistent with Medinan Surahs, while its beginning is more aligned with Meccan Surahs. However, in any case, we regard the whole of it as Medinan, according to the popular view.

Abu Abdillah al-Zanjani, in the exquisite book Tarikh al-Quran, quotes from the catalog of Ibn al-Nadim that Surah At-Taghabun is the twenty-third Surah revealed in Medina. Given that there are 28 Medinan Surahs in total, it must be said that this Surah is among the last ones revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. ^1 In terms of content, this Surah can be divided into several sections:

  1. The beginning of the Surah, which discusses Monotheism (Tawhid) and the attributes and actions of Allah.

  2. Following that, utilizing the knowledge of Allah, it warns people to be mindful of their hidden and manifest deeds and not to forget the fate of previous nations.

  3. In another section of the Surah, the discussion is about the Resurrection (Ma'ad). It mentions that the Day of Judgment is the "Day of Mutual Loss and Gain" (Taghabun), where one group is deprived and loses out while another group wins (the name of the Surah is derived from this).

  4. In another part, it commands obedience to Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and strengthens the foundations of the principle of Prophethood.

  5. The final section of the Surah encourages people to spend (Infaq) in the way of Allah and warns them against being deceived by wealth, children, and spouses, ending the Surah with the names and attributes of Allah, just as it began.

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever recites Surah At-Taghabun, sudden death shall be repelled from him." ^1

And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah At-Taghabun in an obligatory prayer, it will be an intercessor for him on the Day of Resurrection and a just witness before the One who permits its testimony; then it shall not leave him until he enters Paradise." [^2]

It is obvious that this recitation must be accompanied by reflection—a reflection that reflects its content in action—so that all these effects and blessings may follow.

^1 and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 296.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ وَ هُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (1) هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ فَمِنْكُمْ كافِرٌ وَ مِنْكُمْ مُؤْمِنٌ وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (2) خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ صَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ وَ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ (3) يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَ ما تُعْلِنُونَ وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ (4) أَ لَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ فَذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ (5) ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُ كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَقالُوا أَ بَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنا فَكَفَرُوا وَ تَوَلَّوْا وَ اسْتَغْنَى اللَّهُ وَ اللَّهُ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ (6)

Commentary

He is Aware of the Secrets Hidden Within the Breasts

This Surah also begins with the glorification of Allah—the Allah who is the Owner and Ruler of the entire universe and is Powerful over all things. It says: "Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah" (يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ).

Then it adds: "To Him belongs the sovereignty" (لَهُ الْمُلْكُ). "And (for this reason) all praise also returns to His Pure Essence" (وَ لَهُ الْحَمْدُ). "And He is Powerful over all things" (وَ هُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Since we have repeatedly spoken about the universal glorification by the beings of the world and have provided the various interpretations mentioned for this subject, there is no need for repetition. This glorification and praise are, in fact, the necessary consequence of His power over everything and His ownership of everything, for all His attributes of Beauty and Majesty are hidden within these two matters.

Then, it points to the matter of creation, which is a requirement of Power, saying: "He is the One who created you" (هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ).

And He gave you the blessing of freedom and choice; therefore, "some of you are disbelievers and some of you are believers" (فَمِنْكُمْ كافِرٌ وَ مِنْكُمْ مُؤْمِنٌ). ^1

^1: The mention of the "fa" of consequence here is not because faith and disbelief are created by Allah, but because following creation, He gave man freedom of will, and the consequence of that is the emergence of two groups: believers and disbelievers.

In this way, the market of divine trial and testing became heated, and meanwhile, "Allah is Seer of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

Then, it continues the issue of "creation" with more explanation and by pointing to the purpose of creation in the next verse, saying: "Allah created the heavens and the earth with truth" (خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ). Both in their creation, there is a truthful and precise system, and they possess a wise purpose and truthful interests, just as He said in verse 27 of Surah Sad: "And We did not create the heaven and the earth and what is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who disbelieve."

Next, it addresses the creation of "man" and invites us from the "horizons" (external world) to the "souls" (internal world), adding: "He formed you and made your forms beautiful" (وَ صَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ).

He gave man an adorned appearance and a refined inner self, a radiant intellect, and a powerful mind, and He created within his being examples of everything that exists in the entire universe, such that the "macrocosm" is summarized in this "microcosm."

But as it says at the end of the verse: "And to Him is the final return" (وَ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ). Yes, man, who is a part of the collection of the universe, is aligned in terms of creation with the purposeful nature of the whole world; he began moving from the lowest stages and advances toward the Infinite, which is the Endless Being of Truth and proximity to Allah.

The expression "He made your forms beautiful" (فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ) includes both the outward form and the inward form, both in terms of body and soul. Truly, a brief look at the creation of the human body and soul shows that he is the most beautiful phenomenon of the universe, and Allah has displayed a marvelous power and reached the pinnacle of perfection in creating this being!

And since man was created for a great purpose, he must be constantly under the supervision of the Lord—the Lord who is aware of his inner and outer self. Therefore, in the next verse, it says: "Allah knows what is in the heavens and the earth, and He knows what you conceal and what you declare, and Allah is Aware of what is within the breasts" (يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَ ما تُعْلِنُونَ وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

This verse is a depiction of the infinite knowledge of Allah in three stages: first, His knowledge of all beings in the heavens and the earth; then, His knowledge of all the deeds of humans and what they hide or reveal; and in the third stage, it specifically emphasizes internal beliefs, the nature of intentions, and what governs the heart and soul of man.

Knowledge of this vast Divine awareness has an extraordinary educational effect on man and warns him that whoever you are and wherever you reach, and whatever belief you have in your head, whatever intention in your heart, and whatever character within your soul, all are manifest before the infinite knowledge of Truth. Certainly, attention to this reality is exceptionally effective in the reform and education of man, and these are teachings that prepare man to reach the goal of creation and the law of perfection.

And since one of the most effective means of education and methods of warning is drawing attention to the fate of previous nations and peoples, the next verse casts a brief look at their lives, then addresses mankind, saying: "Has there not come to you the news of those who disbelieved before? So they tasted the evil consequence of their conduct, and for them is a painful punishment" (أَ لَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ فَذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ). ^1

You pass by their afflicted and ruined cities on your path toward Sham and other regions; you see with your own eyes the result of their disbelief, oppression, and rebellion, and you read their news in history—those whose scroll of life was folded by storms and floods, or lightning set fire to the harvest of their lives, or devastating earthquakes swallowed them into the earth, or a heavy gale tossed their bodies like straw in every direction. This was their worldly punishment, and in the Hereafter, a painful punishment awaits them as well.

The next verse points to the primary source of this painful fate, adding: "That was because their messengers used to come to them with clear proofs and miracles, but they said out of pride and arrogance: 'Shall mere humans guide us?'" Is such a thing possible? (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُ كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَقالُوا أَ بَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنا). And with this hollow logic, they rose in opposition to them "and disbelieved and turned away" (فَكَفَرُوا وَ تَوَلَّوْا).

"While Allah was self-sufficient, needing neither their faith nor their obedience" (وَ اسْتَغْنَى اللَّهُ). And if He made them duty-bound to faith, obedience, and the avoidance of sin, it was only for their own benefit and their happiness and salvation in this world and the next. Yes, "Allah is Self-Sufficient and Worthy of all praise" (وَ اللَّهُ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ).

If all created beings were to disbelieve, not a speck of dust would settle on the hem of His Majesty, just as if all creatures were believers and obedient to His command, nothing would be added to His Glory. It is we who are in need of these educational, constructive, and perfecting programs.

The expression "Allah was self-sufficient" (وَ اسْتَغْنَى اللَّهُ) is so absolute that it expresses His independence from everything, even from the faith and obedience of humans, so that it is not imagined that all this emphasis and insistence is because the benefit of this obedience returns to Allah. He never created the creatures to gain a profit; He wanted to bestow His generosity upon His servants.

Some have mentioned another interpretation for the sentence "وَ اسْتَغْنَى اللَّهُ," which is that Allah sent so many clear signs, proofs, exhortations, and sufficient advice to them that He was independent of providing anything else.

Ayahs

زَعَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن لَّن يُبْعَثُوا۟ ۚ قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِمَا عَمِلْتُمْ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٧)
The faithless claim that they will not be resurrected. Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did, and that is easy for Allah.’ (7)
فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلنُّورِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلْنَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٨)
So have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the light, which We have sent down, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (8)
يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْجَمْعِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلتَّغَابُنِ ۗ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ وَيُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٩)
When He will you bring you together for the Day of Gathering, it will be a day of privation [and regret]. As for those who have faith in Allah and act righteously, He shall absolve them of their misdeeds and admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. That is the great success. (9)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٠)
But as for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they will be the inmates of the Fire, to remain in it [forever], and it is an evil destination. (10)

Commentary

The Day of Mutual Loss and the Manifestation of Regrets

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the purposeful nature of creation, these verses present the issue of the Resurrection and the Afterlife, which serves as a completion to the discussion on the purpose of human creation.

It begins by addressing the baseless claim of the deniers of the Resurrection, stating:

"The disbelievers claim that they will never be resurrected" (زَعَمَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنْ لَنْ يُبْعَثُوا).

The word za'ama (زَعَمَ) is derived from the root za'm (زَعْم), meaning a statement in which there is a possibility or certainty of falsehood; it is also sometimes applied to a vain or false assumption. In the verse under discussion, the first meaning is intended.

From the words of some lexicographers, it is also understood that this root has been used to mean "reporting" or "informing" in an absolute sense [^1], although it is inferred from the usage of this word and the words of commentators that this term is tinged with the concept of lying. Hence, it has been said: "Everything has a nickname, and za'm is the nickname for a lie."

In any case, following this statement, the Quran commands the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Say: 'Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did. And that is easy for Allah'" (قُلْ بَلى‌ وَ رَبِّي لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِما عَمِلْتُمْ وَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ).

In reality, it first negates the baseless claim of the deniers of the Resurrection with a decisive tone—an emphasized tone coupled with an oath, which reflects the firm conviction of the speaker, namely the Prophet. Then, it provides an argument for it with the phrase "And that is easy for Allah." This is because we know the most significant doubt of the deniers of the Resurrection was how decayed and pulverized bones could return to a new life. The above verse states: when the matter is in the hands of the Almighty Allah, there will be no problem, for He brought them into existence from non-existence in the beginning, and reviving the dead is simpler relative to that.

In fact, according to some, the oath "by my Lord" (وَ رَبِّي) is itself a subtle reference to the proof of the Resurrection. This is because the "Lordship" (Rububiyyah) of Allah necessitates that He does not leave this evolutionary movement of man futile within the confines of this worthless worldly life. In other words, unless we accept the issue of the Resurrection, the Lordship of Allah regarding man and his upbringing and perfection would have no meaning.

Some commentators consider the phrase "And that is easy for Allah" to refer specifically to Allah informing humans of their deeds in the Resurrection, as mentioned in the preceding clause. However, it appears that it refers to the entire content of the verse (the principle of the Resurrection and its corollary, which is the matter of informing about deeds—an informing that serves as a prelude to reckoning and recompense).

In the next verse, it draws the following conclusion: "Now that a Resurrection is certainly in store, believe in Allah and His Messenger and the Light which We have sent down" (فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ وَ النُّورِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلْنا). And know that "Allah is All-Aware of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

In this way, He commands them to prepare themselves for the Resurrection through faith and righteous deeds—faith in three principles: "Allah," "the Prophet," and "the Quran," within which other principles are also included.

The description of the Quran as "Light" (Nur) has appeared in numerous verses, and the expression "We have sent down" (anzalna) is another witness to this. Although in several narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, "Light" in the verse under discussion is interpreted as the existence of the "Imam" عليه السلام, this interpretation may be because the existence of the Imam عليه السلام is considered the practical embodiment of the Book of Allah. Hence, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imam عليه السلام are sometimes referred to as the "Speaking Quran." In the conclusion of one of these narrations from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the Imams:

Wa hum alladhina yunawwiruna quluba al-mu'minin "They are those who bestow light and radiance upon the hearts of the believers." [^1]

The next verse proceeds to describe the Day of Resurrection, saying: "This resurrection, rising, reckoning, and recompense will be on the Day He will assemble you for the Day of Assembly" (يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ الْجَمْعِ‌) [^1]. Yes, one of the names of the Resurrection is "The Day of Assembly" (Yawm al-Jam'), which has been repeatedly mentioned in the verses of the Quran with various expressions. For instance, in verses 49 and 50 of Surah al-Waqi'ah, we read: "Say: 'Indeed, the former and the latter [generations] will surely be gathered together for the appointment of a known Day.'" From this, it is clearly understood that the resurrection of all humans occurs on a single day.

Then it adds: "That is the Day of Mutual Loss" (ذلِكَ يَوْمُ التَّغابُنِ‌).

It is the day when the "winner" (ghabin) and the "loser" (maghbun) are identified; the day when it becomes clear who, in their trade in the world, fell into loss, detriment, and ruin.

It is the day when the people of Hell see their empty places in Paradise and feel regret, and the people of Paradise see their empty places in Hell and rejoice. For it is stated in a Hadith that every human has a place in Paradise and a place in Hell; if he goes to Paradise, his place in Hell is handed over to the people of Hell, and if he goes to Hell, his place in Paradise is handed over to the people of Paradise [^2]. The expression "inheritance" (irth) in the verses of the Quran in this regard likely refers to this very matter.

Thus, another name for the Resurrection is "The Day of Mutual Loss" (Yawm al-Taghabun), the day of the manifestation of losses [^1]. Then, describing the state of the believers on that day, it adds:

"And whoever believes in Allah and does righteous deeds, He will remove from him his misdeeds and admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow, to abide therein forever. That is the great success" (وَ مَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللَّهِ وَ يَعْمَلْ صالِحاً يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّئاتِهِ وَ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ‌).

In this way, when the two primary conditions—namely faith and righteous deeds—are fulfilled, these great blessings will follow: the forgiveness of sins, which occupies the human mind more than anything else, and after being purified from sin, entry into eternal Paradise and attaining great success. Therefore, everything revolves around the axis of faith and righteous deeds, and these are the primary assets. These are the ones who, on that "Day of Mutual Loss," are not only not losers but have reached a great victory and supreme success.

Then it adds: "But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs—those are the companions of the Fire, abiding eternally therein; and wretched is the destination" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

Here too, the factors of wretchedness are counted as two things: "disbelief" (kufr) and "denial of Divine signs," which are the opposites of "faith" and "righteous deeds." Consequently, there the talk is of eternal Paradise, and here of perpetual Hell; there is great success, and here is a wretched destination and a deadly end!

The only differences seen between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell in these two verses are: first, regarding the people of Paradise, the forgiveness of sins is mentioned before anything else, which is not raised for the people of Hell. Second, there, the abiding in Paradise is emphasized with the word "forever" (abada), whereas for the people of Hell, it suffices with the mention of abiding and eternity.

This difference in expression might be because among the people of Hell, there may be disbelievers who mixed the matters of faith and disbelief and might eventually be released from punishment due to their faith, or it may be an indication of His mercy prevailing over His wrath.

However, some commentators believe that abada was omitted in the second sentence because it was already mentioned in the first.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, root za'm. [^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 341. [^1]: Yawma yajma'ukum may be related to latuba'athunna, the phrase latunabba'unna, or khabir. The possibility has also been given that it relates to an omitted verb like udhkur (remember), but the latter seems unlikely; rather, it fits one of the previous possibilities. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 532. [^1]: Taghabun is from the form of tafa'ul, and is usually said of actions that are bilateral, such as conflict (ta'arud) and crowding (tizahum)... This meaning regarding the Resurrection may be such that the conflict between the group of believers and disbelievers manifests its result in the Resurrection; in truth, the Day of Resurrection is the day of the manifestation of mutual loss. It is also understood from the words of some lexicographers that the tafa'ul form is not always in this sense, and here it means the manifestation of loss (ghubn) (Mufradat al-Raghib, root ghabn).

Ayahs

مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١١)
No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave. Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (11)
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٢)
Obey Allah and obey the Apostle; but if you turn away, then Our Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (12)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٣)
Allah—there is no god except Him—in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (13)

Commentary

All Afflictions Are by His Command!

In the first verse under discussion, the Quran points to a general principle regarding the afflictions and painful events of this world. This is perhaps because the existence of suffering has often been used as a pretext by disbelievers to deny justice in this world, or because there are always obstacles on the path to realizing faith and righteous deeds that a believer cannot overcome without steadfastness. Thus, the connection between these verses and the preceding ones becomes clear.

First, It says: "No affliction befalls except by Allah's permission" (مَا أَصَابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

Undoubtedly, all events in this world occur by Allah's permission, for according to the principle of "Unity of Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali), nothing in the entire universe happens without the will of the Truth. However, since afflictions have always occupied minds as a question mark, they are specifically emphasized here.

Of course, "permission" here refers to Allah's creative will (iradah takwini), not His legislative will (iradah tashri'i).

A significant question arises here: many of these afflictions occur through the oppression of tyrants and the will of despots, or a person may become entangled in them due to their own negligence, poor judgment, or wrongdoing. Are all of these by Allah's permission?! In response to this question, it must be said that from the sum of the verses in the Holy Quran regarding afflictions, it appears that afflictions are of two types:

  1. Afflictions that are inherent to the nature of human life, in which human will has the least influence, such as death and certain painful natural events.
  2. Afflictions in which humans have played a role in some way.

Regarding the first category, the Quran says they all occur by Allah's permission, and regarding the second category, it says they befall you because of your own actions [1]!

[1]: Referring to Surah Al-Hadid verse 22, Surah Ash-Shura verse 30, and Surah Al-Imran verse 165 makes this matter completely clear. Further explanation on this subject is provided in Volume 23 under the verses of Surah Al-Hadid.

Therefore, no one can use the excuse that all afflictions are from God to remain silent in the face of oppressors and refrain from struggle; nor can they use this excuse to stop fighting diseases, pests, poverty, and ignorance.

Of course, there is another point here: even in afflictions where the human himself plays a role, the effectiveness of these causes is from Allah and by His permission and command; if He wills, any cause becomes colorless and ineffective.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, He gives glad tidings to the believers: "And whoever believes in Allah, He guides his heart" (so that he does not kneel before afflictions, does not despair, and does not wail or become restless) (وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ).

When this divine guidance reaches a person, he becomes grateful in times of blessing, patient in times of affliction, and submissive to the divine decree.

Certainly, "guidance of the heart" has a broad meaning, of which "patience," "gratitude," "contentment," "submission," and saying "Indeed we belong to Allah and indeed to Him we shall return" (إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ رَاجِعُونَ) are each branches. The fact that some commentators have mentioned only one of these subjects does not encompass the entire meaning of the verse but is rather an explanation of a clear manifestation. At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is All-Knowing of all things" (وَاللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

This expression can be a brief allusion to the philosophy of afflictions and trials: that Allah, based on His infinite knowledge and awareness, occasionally creates afflictions in the lives of His servants for their upbringing, as a wake-up call, and to combat any form of pride and negligence, so they do not fall into a deep sleep, do not forget their position in the world, and do not resort to rebellion and transgression.

Since the knowledge of the Origin and the Return (Ma'ad), which was established in the previous verses, has the inevitable effect of striving toward the obedience of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the next verse adds:

"And obey Allah and obey the Messenger" (وَأَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ).

It goes without saying that obedience to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله is also a branch of obedience to Allah, for he says nothing of his own accord. The repetition of "obey" (ati'u) here indicates that these two are not parallel to each other, but rather one stems from the other. Furthermore, obedience to Allah relates to the principles of laws and divine legislation, while obedience to the Messenger relates to interpretations and executive matters; therefore, one is the root and the other is the branch.

Then He says: "But if you turn away and do not obey, he is never commanded to compel you; the duty of Our Messenger is only the clear delivery [of the message]" (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَى رَسُولِنَا الْبَلَاغُ الْمُبِينُ) [1]. Yes, he is responsible for delivering the message of truth; thereafter, your affair is with Allah. This expression is a kind of serious and implicit threat.

In the next verse, He refers to the issue of Monotheism in Worship (Tawhid in 'Ubudiyyah), which serves as a reason for the necessity of obedience, saying: "Allah—there is no deity except Him" (اللَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

[1]: The conditional sentence "فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ" (if you turn away) has an omitted apodosis (jazaa'), which is estimated as: "if you turn away, he has fulfilled his duty"—or—"if you turn away, he will not force you into faith"—or—"there is no blame upon him," and the like.

"And since it is so, let the believers rely only upon Allah" (وَعَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ).

None besides Him is worthy of worship, for ownership, power, knowledge, and self-sufficiency all belong to Him, and whatever others possess, they have from Him. For this reason, one must not bow in submission or veneration before anyone other than Him, and likewise, for the solution to any problem, one must seek help from Him and rely only upon Him.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ عَدُوًّا لَّكُمْ فَٱحْذَرُوهُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَعْفُوا۟ وَتَصْفَحُوا۟ وَتَغْفِرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤)
O you who have faith! Indeed, you have enemies among your spouses and children; so beware of them. Yet if you excuse, forbear, and forgive, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (14)
إِنَّمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٥)
Rather, your possessions, and children are a test, and Allah—with Him is a great reward! (15)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ خَيْرًا لِّأَنفُسِكُمْ ۗ وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٦)
So be wary of Allah, as much as you can, and listen and obey, and spend [in the way of Allah]; that is better for yourselves. Those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous. (16)
إِن تُقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٧)
If you lend Allah a good loan, He shall multiply it for you and forgive you, and Allah is all-appreciative, all-forbearing, (17)
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٨)
Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (18)

Occasion of Revelation

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the verse "إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ..." (Indeed, among your wives...): He said: This refers to when some men intended to emigrate (perform Hijrah), and their sons and wives would cling to them, saying: "We adjure you by Allah not to emigrate, for if you leave, we will be left without a guardian after you." Some would accept this and stay behind. Thus, the above verse was revealed, warning them against accepting such proposals and obeying children and wives in these matters. However, others paid no heed and left, but they would say to their families: "By Allah, if you do not emigrate with us and later come to us in (Dar al-Hijrah) Medina, we will absolutely pay no attention to you." But they were commanded that whenever their families joined them, they should forget the past. The phrase "وَ إِنْ تَعْفُوا وَ تَصْفَحُوا وَ تَغْفِرُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ" (And if you pardon and overlook and forgive, then indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) refers to this very meaning. [1]

[1]: Tafsir "Ali ibn Ibrahim" as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 342 (Hadith 20). The same meaning is narrated more briefly in Tafsir "al-Durr al-Manthur" and "other commentaries" from Ibn Abbas, but none are as comprehensive as the above narration.

Commentary

Your Wealth and Children are a Means of Trial for You

Since the previous verses commanded unconditional obedience to Allah and His Messenger, and since one of the major obstacles in this path is excessive affection for wealth, spouses, and children, the verses under discussion warn the Muslims in this regard.

First, it says: "O you who have believed! Indeed, among your wives and your children are enemies to you, so beware of them" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ وَ أَوْلادِكُمْ عَدُوًّا لَكُمْ فَاحْذَرُوهُمْ).

Certainly, the signs of this enmity are not few; sometimes you intend to take a positive action like emigration, and they cling to you, preventing you from this great grace. Sometimes they await your death to take possession of your wealth, and things of this nature.

Undoubtedly, not all children are like this, nor are all spouses; therefore, the verse uses the partitive "min" (among/some of) to indicate that only some of them are such, so be watchful of them.

Of course, this enmity is sometimes in the guise of friendship and with the intention of service, and sometimes it is truly carried out with ill intent and the purpose of hostility, or for the sake of their own interests.

The important point is that when a person stands at a crossroads where one path leads toward Allah and the other toward wife and children, and these two happen to diverge, he must not hesitate in his decision-making; he must prioritize the pleasure of the Truth (Allah) over everything else, for the salvation of this world and the hereafter lies therein.

Hence, we read in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 23: "O you who have believed, do not take your fathers and your brothers as allies if they have preferred disbelief over belief. And whoever does so among you—then it is those who are the wrongdoers" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا آباءَكُمْ وَ إِخْوانَكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ إِنِ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْكُفْرَ عَلَى الْإِيمانِ وَ مَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

However, since this command might become an excuse for harshness, vengeance, and excess on the part of fathers and husbands, the verse immediately follows with a moderating instruction: "And if you pardon and overlook and forgive, then indeed Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ إِنْ تَعْفُوا وَ تَصْفَحُوا وَ تَغْفِرُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Therefore, if they regret their actions and come forward to apologize, or if they join you after the emigration, do not drive them away; adopt pardon and forgiveness, just as you expect Allah to do the same for you.

In the story of the Slander (Ifk) against "Aisha" in the commentary of Surah An-Nur, we read: When the rumors spread by the scandal-mongers escalated in Medina, some believers whose relatives were among the ranks of the rumor-mongers swore that they would cut off all financial aid to them. Verse 22 of that same Surah was revealed, saying: "And let not those of virtue among you and wealth swear not to give [aid] to their relatives and the needy and the emigrants for the cause of Allah, and let them pardon and overlook":

"Do you not love that Allah should forgive you?" (أَ لا تُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ).

As for the difference between "Afw" (pardon), "Safh" (overlooking), and "Ghufran" (forgiveness), it becomes clear by considering their linguistic meanings that they describe the hierarchy of forgiving a sin. "Afw" means to forgo punishment; "Safh" is a higher level, meaning to cease all blame; and "Ghufran" means to cover the sin and consign it to oblivion.

In this way, while being firm in preserving their principles of faith and not submitting to wives and children who invite them to the wrong path, the believers must not withhold love, pardon, and forgiveness at all stages as much as they can, for all of these are means for education and returning them to the path of obedience to Allah.

In the next verse, it points to another general principle regarding wealth and children, saying: "Your wealth and your children are only a trial" (إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَ أَوْلادِكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ). And if you succeed in this field of trial, "Allah has with Him a great reward" (وَ اللَّهُ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ).

In the previous verse, the talk was only about the enmity of "some" spouses and children toward a person, diverting him from the path of obedience to Allah and leading him to sin and sometimes to disbelief. But here, the talk is about "all" children and wealth being a means of trial for man.

In reality, for the purpose of human development, Allah constantly places man in the hot furnaces of examination and tests him with various matters. However, these two (wealth and children) constitute the most important means of his examination, because the attraction of wealth on one hand and the love and affection for children on the other create such a powerful pull in man that in cases where the pleasure of Allah is separated from their pleasure, man is placed under severe pressure.

The use of "Innama" (only/verily), which is usually for restriction, shows that these two subjects are more than anything else a means of trial. For this reason, we read in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that he said:

"None of you should say: 'O Allah, I seek refuge in You from trial (fitnah),' for there is no one who is not involved in a trial. But whoever seeks refuge, let him seek refuge from the misleading trials, for Allah the Sublimely Exalted says: 'And know that your wealth and your children are a trial.'" [1] The same meaning with a slight difference is seen in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 28 (what is mentioned at the end of the words of the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام and the above narration is the expression used in Surah Al-Anfal; take note).

Here, many commentators and traditionists have narrated that one day the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله was delivering a sermon from the pulpit. Hasan and Husayn عليهما السلام, who were two young children at the time, entered wearing red shirts. Occasionally, while walking, they would stumble and fall. As soon as the eyes of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله fell upon them, he interrupted his sermon, descended from the pulpit, took them in his arms, brought them up to the pulpit, sat them on his lap, and then said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, spoke the truth when He said: 'Your wealth and your children are only a trial.' When I looked at these two children walking and stumbling, I could not remain patient until I interrupted my speech and picked them up." After that, he continued his sermon. [2]

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, No. 93. [2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 301 (under the verses discussed). The same hadith is narrated by "Qurtubi" in his commentary, and in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," "Fi Zilal," and "Al-Mizan" with minor differences.

It should be noted that "fitnah" and trial can sometimes be a trial of good and sometimes a trial of evil. Here, it can be a trial of good in the sense that Allah wants to test His Prophet: while occupied with a sermon, and that too upon the pulpit, is it possible for him to be heedless of the state of these two children who are the dear ones of Zahra عليه السلام and each of whom will hold a lofty station in the future? Or will the awe and majesty of the sermon not prevent the expression of emotion and love? Otherwise, it is certain that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would never be heedless of the remembrance of Allah and the fulfillment of the heavy responsibility of preaching and guidance for the sake of love for children.

In any case, this action of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was a warning to all Muslims to recognize the status of these two children of Ali عليه السلام and Fatima عليه السلام.

Thus, in a hadith narrated in famous Sunni sources, we read that "Bara' ibn Azib" (the famous companion) says:

"I saw Hasan ibn Ali on the shoulder of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while he was saying: 'O Allah, I love him, so love him.'" [1] In other narrations, it is mentioned that sometimes Husayn عليه السلام would come and climb onto the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله back during prostration, and the Prophet would not prevent him! [2] All of these demonstrate the greatness of the status of these two great Imams.

[1]: "Sahih Muslim," Vol. 4, p. 1883 (Chapter on the Virtues of Hasan and Husayn عليهما السلام, Hadith 58). [2]: "Bihar," Vol. 43, p. 296, Hadith 57.

In the next verse, as a conclusion, it says: "Now that it is so, fear Allah as much as you are able, and listen and obey and spend [in the way of Allah]; it is better for your souls" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ وَ اسْمَعُوا وَ أَطِيعُوا وَ أَنْفِقُوا خَيْراً لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ).

First, it commands the avoidance of sins (since Taqwa mostly refers to abstaining from sin), and then it commands obedience to the command and "listening" which is the prelude to this obedience. Among the acts of obedience, it specifically emphasizes the issue of spending (Infaq), which is one of the most important divine trials. Finally, it says the benefit of all this returns to yourselves.

Some commentators have interpreted "khayran" (better/good) to mean "wealth," which is a means of achieving positive deeds, just as it appears with this meaning in the verse of the will:

"It is prescribed for you when death approaches any one of you if he leaves wealth (khayran) that he should make a bequest for parents and next of kin according to what is acceptable" (Baqarah-180).

A group has also interpreted "khayran" in a broad sense, not considering it a modifier for "spending" (infaq), but rather relating it to the entire verse, saying: the meaning is that obedience to all these commands is to your benefit. (This interpretation seems more appropriate). [1] It is also necessary to mention that the command to have Taqwa to the extent of one's ability does not contradict verse 102 of Surah Al-Imran, which says: "Fear Allah as He should be feared" (اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ). Rather, these two complement each other.

[1]: According to the first interpretation, "khayran" is the object of "anfiqu" (spend), and according to the second interpretation, it is the predicate (khabar) for a hidden verb, and the estimation is "yaku khayran lakum" (it will be better for you).

Both complement each other because in one place it says: "Fear Allah as much as you are able," and in another place it says: "Fulfill the right of Taqwa." It is certain that fulfilling the right of Taqwa is within the measure of human power and ability, for a duty beyond one's capacity is meaningless. The goal is for man to exert his utmost effort in this path.

Therefore, those who considered the verse under discussion as an abrogation of the verse in Surah Al-Imran are in error.

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis on the issue of spending, it says: "And whoever is protected from the stinginess and greed of his own soul—it is those who will be the successful" (وَ مَنْ يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

"Shuhh" means stinginess combined with greed, and we know that these two base qualities are among the greatest obstacles to human salvation and the greatest barriers to spending and good deeds. If a person clings to Divine grace and asks Him with his whole being, and strives in self-construction and purification of the soul to be saved from these two vices, he has guaranteed his happiness.

Although in some narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام it is stated:

"Whoever pays the Zakat has been protected from the stinginess of his soul," [1] it is clear that this is one of the vivid examples of abandoning stinginess and greed, not its entire meaning.

In another hadith, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام performed the circumambulation (Tawaf) of the House of Allah from night until morning, constantly saying:

"O Allah! Protect me from the stinginess of my soul." One of his companions said: "May I be sacrificed for you, tonight I did not hear you make any supplication other than this." He replied: "What thing is more important than the stinginess and greed of the soul, while Allah says: 'And whoever is protected from the stinginess of his soul—it is those who will be the successful'"? [1]

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 301. [1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 346.

Then, to encourage spending and prevent stinginess and greed of the soul, it says: "If you loan Allah a goodly loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you. And Allah is Most Appreciative and Forbearing" (إِنْ تُقْرِضُوا اللَّهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً يُضاعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ).

What an amazing expression that has been repeated many times in the Holy Quran regarding "spending in the way of Allah"! God, who is the Creator of the essence and branches of our existence, the Giver of all blessings, and the original Owner of all kingdoms, asks us for a loan! And in return, He promises "multiplied reward" and forgiveness, and He also thanks us. No grace or love higher than this can be imagined, and no nobility or mercy beyond this is possible. What are we and what do we have that we should give Him a loan? And furthermore, why should we receive such a great reward?! Are these not all signs of the importance of the issue of spending on one hand, and Allah's endless grace toward His servants on the other?

"Qard" (loan) originally means to cut or sever, and when accompanied by the word "Hasan" (goodly), it refers to separating wealth from oneself and giving it in the way of good.

"Yuda'ifuhu" (He multiplies it) is from the root "di'f." As we have said before, it does not only mean double, but includes many times over, which in the case of "spending" (infaq) has been mentioned in the Quran as up to seven hundred times and more! (Baqarah-261).

Additionally, the phrase "وَ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ" (and He will forgive you) is evidence that spending is one of the factors for the forgiveness of sins.

The use of "Shakur" (Most Appreciative), which is one of the attributes of Allah, is evidence that Allah thanks His servants by means of great rewards. His being "Halim" (Forbearing) refers to the issue of forgiving sins and not hastening the punishment of servants.

Finally, in the last verse, it says: "He is the Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Exalted in Might, the Wise" (عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ). He is aware of the deeds of the servants, especially their spending, in secret and in public. If He asks them for a loan, it is not out of need or lack of power, but for the sake of the perfection of grace and love. And if He promises them all these rewards in return for spending, this too is the requirement of His wisdom.

Thus, the five attributes of Allah mentioned in this verse and the previous one all have a kind of connection to the issue of spending in His way. However, paying attention to these five attributes of Allah, in addition to the issue of encouraging spending, makes man more determined in the matter of obeying the Lord in general and refraining from any kind of sin, and grants him strength of heart, willpower, and the spirit of Taqwa.

Key Point: A Meaningful Hadith

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam, it is stated:

"No newborn is born except that in the lattices of his head are written five verses from Surah At-Taghabun"! [1] It is possible that this refers to these last five verses of the Surah which speak about wealth and children. The writing of these five verses in the lattices of the head refers to the certainty of this matter; that is, the content of these verses is true regarding all the children of Adam without exception.

The term "shababik," the plural of "shubbak," meaning "lattice," might refer to the bones of the head whose pieces are interlocked, or it might refer to the neural networks of the brain. In any case, it is evidence of the existence of these psychological traits in humans.

O Allah! Help us in this great trial of wealth, children, and spouses. O Lord! Keep us away from stinginess, greed, and the "shuhh" of the soul, for whomever You keep away from it is among the people of salvation and success.

[1]: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 24.

O God! On the Day of Resurrection, the day when the loss and deprivation of the sinful servants becomes apparent, keep us away from this "Taghabun" (mutual loss) under the shelter of Your grace. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah At-Taghabun, 22nd of the Blessed Ramadan, 1406 AH.
